images.caption: Figure 1:Overview ofRankJudge, a benchmarkgeneratorfor multi-turn judge evaluation.

Figure 2:(Left) Cumulative fraction of samples annotated by humans as having ambiguous or noisy labels among conversation pairs with BT-Elo at mostxx. Both problematic types concentrate at the top of the difficulty distribution while easier pairs are almost entirely unambiguous. (Right) Fine-tuningQwen3.5-4Bon the top-20% slice underperforms training on the cleaner slices.

Figure 3:Elo scores of2121judges on the combined dataset. Black circles give the combined Elo with95%95\%CI; colored markers show per-domain Elo scores. Tick-label color denotes proprietary (blue) vs. open-source (orange) judges. Table4in AppendixA.1gives detailed per-domain results.

Figure 4:Judge Elo against per-match compute. Top: mean completion tokens per match (linear). Bottom: mean cost per match in USD (log). Markers are coloured by provider and the dashed grey step line traces the empirical Pareto frontier.

Figure 5:Per-class prediction bias for each judge. Each cell gives the difference in percentage points between the judge’s predicted share of the assistant failure type and the ground-truth share. Red indicates over-prediction, while blue shows under-prediction. Columns are sorted by Elo.

Figure 6:(a) Judge Elo with and without failure-type correctness as a criterion. (b) Accuracy vs. mean completion tokens for four default-prompt baselines and fouropus-4.7prompt revisions; the dashed step is the empirical Pareto frontier. (c) Rank correlation between the full-sample judge ranking and rankings recomputed on uniform subsamples.

Figure 7:Per-pair Elo on the combined dataset, grouped by (a) assistant failure type and (b) user behavior type. Categories are sorted by median Elo; violins show the full distribution, overlaid boxes mark the interquartile range (IQR) with the median in red, and jittered points mark individual pairs.

Figure 8:Same-model preference does not distort the rest of the leaderboard. Each panel is a slope chart of judge Elo ranks under the published pair set generated bygpt-5.5(left axis) against the parallel set generated byopus-4.7(right axis), with ranks recomputed from a Bradley–Terry fit within the panel’s judge subset.

Figure 10:Per-judge confusion of assistant failure type predictions, row-normalized so each row sums to one. Top row: the four highest-Elo judges; bottom row: the four lowest. Class labels abbreviate the seven assistant failure types (SCself_contradiction, EVevasion, DOdisorganized, FAfabricated_answer, IFinstruction_forgetting, NCno_clarification, URunnecessary_refusal).

Figure 11:Per-domain version of Figure6(a). Judge Elo with the full correctness criterion (x-axis) vs. Elo with the failure-type label dropped from the loss (y-axis), refit independently within each domain. Each marker is a judge; the dashed line marksy=xy=xand the red line is the least-squares fit. Per-panel Spearmanρ\rhoand Kendallτ\tauare computed on the underlying Elo scores.

Figure 12:Per-domain version of Figure6(c). Each panel reports Spearmanρ\rhoand Kendallτ\taubetween the full-sampletop_removedBT ranking and thepartial_obsBT ranking refit on uniform subsamples at fractions{10%,20%,50%,80%}\{10\%,20\%,50\%,80\%\}, computed independently within each domain.

Figure 13:Judge ranks with (x-axis) and without (y-axis) the top-ranked pair removal, one panel per slice. The “with” condition is the publishedtop_removedslice; the “without” condition is the raw filtered slice taken straight from the automatic verification pipeline of AppendixB.7. Each marker is a judge; the dashed line isy=xy=x. Both axes are inverted so that rank 1 sits at the top-right. Per-panel Spearmanρ\rhoand Kendallτ\tauare computed on the underlying Elo scores.

Figure 14:Judge ranks under Bradley–Terry Elo (x-axis) and Empirical Interaction Propagation (y-axis), one panel per slice. Each marker is a judge; the dashed line isy=xy=x. Both axes are inverted so that rank 1 sits at the top-right. Per-panel Spearmanρ\rhoand Kendallτ\tauare computed on the underlying scores.

Figure 15:Pointwise vs. pairwise judging on a100100-pair stratified sample. (a) Each judge’s pointwise Elo plotted against its pairwise Elo recomputed on the same pair set; the dashed line marksy=xy=x. (b) Per-judge pointwise score-gap: the mean Likert score on the good conversation in each pair minus the mean score on the flawed one, sorted descending.

Figure 16:The pair-audit interface used for the human label-noise audit. The annotator inspects the ground-truth metadata, the good and bad plans, the two conversation transcripts (with the injected-flaw turn opened and tagged), the three verifier outputs, and each of the 21 judges’ verdicts and reasoning, then commits one of three labels (clean,ambiguous,noise) along with a free-text note. The screenshot shows the Conversations tab on a representativemlpair.
